title: Alogliptin -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
content: The ADA's Standards of Care in Diabetes currently considers DPP-4 inhibitors an add-on therapy for patients who do not achieve their A1C goal after 3 months of lifestyle modifications and metformin. DPP-4 inhibitors are valuable in both dual and triple therapy. Current guidelines emphasize selecting a second agent based on the patient's RISK OF atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD). If a patient has ASCVD, clinicians should select an agent with proven cardiovascular benefits and mortality reduction. Alogliptin has not demonstrated a reduction in cardiovascular risks or mortality, and concerns exist regarding a potential increased risk of heart failure with DPP-4 inhibitors. Given that nearly half of all patients with T2D develop heart failure during their lifetime, this remains a critical consideration in treatment selection.

title: Lomitapide -- Indications
content: According to the National Lipid Association guidelines, lomitapide can decrease LDL-C levels by up to 50% in patients diagnosed with HoFH who already receive maximum tolerated lipid-lowering therapy and lipoprotein apheresis. [9] According to the 2023 European Atherosclerosis Society Consensus Statement on Homozygous Familial Hypercholesterolaemia, a combination of LDL-C–lowering therapy involving both pharmacologic intervention and lipoprotein apheresis forms the cornerstone of treatment. By incorporating novel and effective therapies such as PCSK9 inhibitors, followed by evinacumab or lomitapide, clinicians can achieve the LDL-C goal and decrease the reliance on lipoprotein apheresis. [10]

content: Exenatide is FDA-indicated to improve glycemic control in adult patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus when used as an adjunct to diet and exercise. It is not recommended as a first-line therapy to treat diabetes. Clinical trials have shown exenatide to be safe and effective when used as either monotherapy or in combination with other diabetic medications. title: Familial Hypercholesterolemia -- Treatment / Management -- Drug Therapies
content: Statins are the standard therapy for familial hypercholesterolemia. All guidelines recommend statins as first-line drugs for patients with FH, with a goal of reaching maximally tolerated doses. [33] The effect of statins has been well studied, and most randomized clinical trials observed a reduction of around 50 percent of the initial untreated LDL levels, as well as a reduction in cardiovascular events. [34] [35] [36] Unfortunately, a large number of patients with FH, even on maximally-tolerated statins alone, either do not show this level of reduction in LDL levels, or they do not reach their goal LDL levels, even with a 50 percent reduction, because their initial LDL levels are so high. [35] Second-line therapies include ezetimibe and PCSK9 inhibitors. Ezetimibe has been showing to add an extra 10 to 30 percent reduction in LDL levels for patients on maximally tolerated statins. [37] [38] Currently, ezetimibe is the drug of choice as the second line in most guidelines. [39] PCSK9 inhibitors have shown a great reduction in LDL levels and cardiovascular outcomes in different randomized clinical trials. [22] [40] In those trials, PCKS9 inhibitors lowered LDL levels by 50 to 60 percent. While exhibiting higher efficacy than ezetimibe, the use of PCSK9 inhibitors is limited by their high cost and the reluctance of insurance companies to approve their use. [41] Hence, PCSK9 inhibitors can be used as second or third-line drugs for patients with familial hypercholesterolemia. Fourth line therapies include mipomersen (mRNA inhibition of apolipoprotein B), lomitapide (microsomal triglyceride transfer protein inhibition), niacin, lipoprotein apheresis, ileal bypass surgery, and liver transplantation. [42] [43] [44] [45] These are usually reserved if the LDL does not reach the target level using statins, ezetimibe, and PCSK9 inhibitors. [44]

title: Lomitapide -- Continuing Education Activity
content: Lomitapide could be beneficial for HoFH patients exhibiting insufficient response to PCSK9 inhibitor treatment, those with atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and a baseline LDL-C of 190 mg/dL or higher, and those who demonstrate insufficient responses to statins, whether used alone or in combination with PCSK9 inhibitors or ezetimibe. Lomitapide rapidly binds to and inhibits microsomal triglyceride transfer protein (MTP) within the endoplasmic reticulum lumen, which impedes the synthesis of lipoproteins containing apo-B in both hepatocytes and enterocytes. This process reduces the production of very low-density lipoprotein and chylomicrons, thereby lowering plasma LDL-C levels. The safety and efficacy of lomitapide have not been established for heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia. This activity elucidates the indications, mechanism of action, pharmacokinetics, adverse event profile, and other relevant interactions essential for interprofessional healthcare team members in treating patients with HoFH and related conditions.

title: Pitavastatin -- Indications
content: Pitavastatin is currently FDA approved for the management of primary dyslipidemia and mixed dyslipidemia as adjunctive therapy to dietary changes to help lower total cholesterol, low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL), apolipoprotein B (Apo B), triglycerides (TG), and to increase high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL). [1] [2] [3] It is also FDA approved to treat heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (HeFH) in pediatric patients over 8 years of age. [4] [5] Unlike other statins, pitavastatin has not been studied in any large randomized controlled study to determine whether its use is associated with decreased cardiovascular events.

title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 174
content: The community defines “DRUGS USED IN DIABETES” as comprising two major super‑classes: **INSULINS AND ANALOGUES** and **BLOOD GLUCOSE‑LOWERING DRUGS, EXCL. INSULINS**. Within INSULINS AND ANALOGUES, the subclass **INSULINS AND ANALOGUES FOR INJECTION, LONG‑ACTING** includes agents such as **INSULIN GLARGINE**, the combination product **INSULIN DEGLUDEC/LIRAGLUTIDE** (which incorporates **LIRAGLUTIDE**), and **INSULIN GLARGINE/LIXISENATIDE** (which incorporates **LIXISENATIDE**). Non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents are organized into several pharmacologic classes. **ALPHA GLUCOSIDASE INHIBITORS** lower blood glucose by delaying carbohydrate absorption. **DIPEPTIDYL PEPTIDASE‑4 (DPP‑4) INHIBITORS**—including **ALOGLIPTIN** and **SITAGLIPTIN** (the latter also listed as a DPP‑4 inhibitor)—are classified under BLOOD GLUCOSE‑LOWERING DRUGS, EXCL. INSULINS. **GLUCAGON‑LIKE PEPTIDE‑1 (GLP‑1) ANALOGUES** comprise **EXENATID**, **LIRAGLUTIDE**, and **LIXISENATIDE**. **BIGUANIDE BLOOD GLUCOSE LOWER DRUGS** represent another subclass; **FENFORMIN (metformin)** is a member, illustrating that biguanides are a subset of the broader non‑insulin glucose‑lowering category. Combination formulations link agents from different classes, exemplified by **SIMVASTATIN/SITAGLIPTIN**, which pairs the statin **SIMVASTATIN** with the DPP‑4 inhibitor **SITAGLIPTIN**. An important drug‑interaction contraindication is noted: **NELFINAVIR MESYLATE** is contraindicated when used with **SIMVASTATIN**. Overall, the taxonomy clarifies that insulin preparations (including long‑acting analogues) and various oral or injectable non‑insulin agents (alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, DPP‑4 inhibitors, GLP‑1 analogues, and biguanides) are organized under the overarching category of diabetes medications, with combination products and specific contraindications guiding safe and effective patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 38
content: The therapeutic category “drugs used in diabetes” comprises insulin‑based products (insulins and analogues) and non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents. Within the insulin family, long‑acting injectable analogues such as insulin glargine, insulin degludec and combination formulations that pair a basal insulin with a rapid‑acting component (e.g., beef insulin and human insulin) are classified as long‑acting or intermediate/long‑acting plus fast‑acting insulins. Fixed‑dose combinations that couple a basal insulin with a glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogue—insulin degludec / liraglutide and insulin glargine / lixisenatide—provide complementary glycaemic control. The GLP‑1 analogue class includes exenatid, liraglutide and lixisenatide. Non‑insulin agents include alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, dipeptidyl peptidase‑4 (DPP‑4) inhibitors (e.g., alogliptin, sitagliptin) and biguanides (e.g., fenformin/metformin). Sitagliptin is incorporated in a fixed‑dose tablet with the statin simvastatin; simvastatin is contraindicated when co‑administered with the antiretroviral nelfinavir mesylate. This taxonomy links basal long‑acting insulin analogues, oral biguanides, DPP‑4 inhibitors and GLP‑1 analogues, highlighting their distinct mechanisms, common combination products, and important drug‑interaction considerations in diabetes management.

title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 171
content: The pharmacologic management of diabetes is organized into two principal categories: insulin and analogue products that replace or augment endogenous insulin, and non‑insulin blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs. Non‑insulin agents include alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, biguanides (metformin and fenformin), dipeptidyl peptidase‑4 (DPP‑4) inhibitors such as alogliptin and sitagliptin (which inhibit incretin degradation), and glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues such as exenatide, liraglutide and lixisenatide (which mimic GLP‑1 to enhance glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, delay gastric emptying and suppress appetite). Simvastatin, although a lipid‑lowering statin, is not a DPP‑4 inhibitor. Within the insulin and analogue hierarchy, long‑acting injectable insulins (e.g., insulin glargine, insulin degludec) are formulated alone or combined with a GLP‑1 analogue—insulin glargine/lixisenatide and insulin degludec/liraglutide—providing basal insulin coverage together with the complementary actions of the GLP‑1 component. Another subclass comprises intermediate‑ or long‑acting insulins mixed with rapid‑acting insulin to achieve both basal and prandial control; historically, beef insulin, a protein‑based insulin derived from bovine sources, is listed in this combined‑insulin category. This taxonomy illustrates that insulin‑based products directly replace deficient hormone, while non‑insulin agents lower glucose through distinct biochemical pathways, and that combination products pairing long‑acting insulins with GLP‑1 analogues represent contemporary strategies to simplify regimens and achieve synergistic glycemic control.

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 110
content: The community describes a network of pharmacologic classes and their interrelations. β‑3‑adrenergic receptor antagonists include 2,3‑dihydrothiopyrano(2,3‑b)pyridine—chemically related to a pyridine scaffold—and L 750355. Lipid‑modifying therapy comprises plain agents such as HMG‑CoA reductase inhibitors (atorvastatin, simvastatin, fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, cerivastatin, pravastatin) and other groups: the antilipidemic benfluorex, nicotinic‑acid and its derivatives (which also exist as a peripheral‑vasodilator form), and bile‑acid sequestrants exemplified by colesevelam. All of these agents belong to the broader cardiovascular system drug category, which additionally contains peripheral vasodilators and enzymes that act as peripheral vasodilators. Simvastatin is formulated in a fixed‑dose combination with sitagliptin and is contraindicated with the HIV protease inhibitor nelfinavir (including nelfinavir mesylate). Colesevelam is contraindicated in patients with الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياس.

title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 63
content: The therapeutic landscape for diabetes is organized into two principal families: insulins and analogues, and blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs that exclude insulins. Insulins and analogues comprise injectable formulations, including long‑acting insulins such as insulin glargine, the combination insulin degludec / liraglutide, and the combination insulin glargine / lixisenatide; the latter two products incorporate the GLP‑1 analogues liraglutide and lixisenatide, respectively. Blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs excl. insulins are divided into several mechanistic subclasses. Alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors lower glucose by delaying carbohydrate absorption. Dipeptidyl‑peptidase‑4 (DPP‑4) inhibitors, a non‑insulin subclass, include alogliptin, sitagliptin, and the fixed‑dose combination simvastatin / sitagliptin. Glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues—exenatide, liraglutide, and lixisenatide—also reduce glucose without containing insulin. Biguanides, another subclass of non‑insulin antihyperglycemic agents, consist of metformin and phenformin (listed as “fenformin”). Together, insulin‑based regimens (both long‑acting and combination products) and the diverse non‑insulin agents—alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, DPP‑4 inhibitors, GLP‑1 analogues, and biguanides—provide complementary mechanisms to achieve optimal glycemic control in patients with diabetes.

title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 37
content: The pharmacologic landscape for diabetes comprises two principal therapeutic families: insulins and analogues (injectable insulin products) and non‑insulin blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs. The insulin‑and‑analogue family includes intermediate‑ or long‑acting insulins, long‑acting basal insulins such as insulin glargine, and fixed‑dose combinations that pair a basal insulin with a glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogue—specifically insulin degludec / liraglutide and insulin glargine / lixisenatide. “Insulin, Beef” is identified as a protein‑based insulin used in mixed‑duration regimens. GLP‑1 analogues, a subclass of non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents, include exenatid (exenatide), liraglutid (liraglutide) and lixisenatide; liraglutide and lixisenatide are the GLP‑1 components of the aforementioned insulin‑GLP‑1 combination products. Non‑insulin glucose‑lowering drugs also encompass enzyme‑inhibitor classes. Alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors belong to this group, as do dipeptidyl peptidase‑4 (DPP‑4) inhibitors, which comprise alogliptin, sitagliptin and the sitagliptin component of the simvastatin / sitagliptin combination product. Biguanides represent another subclass of non‑insulin agents; fenformin is a specific biguanide medication. All entities—insulin preparations, basal‑insulin/GLP‑1 combinations, GLP‑1 receptor agonists, alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, DPP‑4 inhibitors, and biguanides—are classified under blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs (excluding insulins) or under the insulin‑and‑analogue family, illustrating the hierarchical relationships that guide precise therapeutic selection and optimized patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1624
content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin and pitavastatin are all members of the therapeutic class identified as HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This class of agents works by inhibiting the HMG CoA reductase enzyme, which is the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, thereby lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and reducing overall cardiovascular risk. Because they share the same mechanism of action, these statins are commonly used in the management of hyperlipidaemia and for primary or secondary prevention of atherosclerotic disease. Recognizing that atorvastatin, fluvastatin and pitavastatin belong to the same drug class helps clinicians select appropriate lipid‑lowering therapy and anticipate class‑related effects and potential drug interactions.

title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 174
content: DRUGS USED IN DIABETES is an overarching therapeutic class that comprises two major groups: INSULINS AND ANALOGUES and BLOOD GLUCOSE‑LOWERING DRUGS EXCLUDING INSULINS. title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
content: The pharmacotherapy of diabetes is organized into two principal subclasses: insulins and analogues, and non‑insulin blood‑glucose‑lowering agents. Insulins and analogues for injection used in diabetes comprise intermediate‑ or long‑acting formulations that may be combined with fast‑acting agents; this includes the long‑acting subgroup (e.g., insulin glargine, insulin degludec / liraglutide, insulin glargine / lixisenatide) and the intermediate/long‑acting combined with fast‑acting subgroup (e.g., insulin aspart, human, which targets the human INSR protein). Insulin, Beef is classified as a protein within this category. Non‑insulin agents are divided mechanistically into alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, dipeptidyl peptidase‑4 (DPP‑4) inhibitors, glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues, and biguanides. DPP‑4 inhibitors include alogliptin and sitagliptin; sitagliptin is also present in the fixed‑dose combination simvastatin / sitagliptin, which pairs the statin simvastatin with the DPP‑4 inhibitor. GLP‑1 analogues such as liraglutide and lixisenatide are incorporated into combination products with long‑acting insulins (insulin degludec / liraglutide and insulin glargine / lixisenatide) to enhance glycaemic control. Biguanides, represented by phenformin and metformin, lower glucose by reducing hepatic production and increasing peripheral insulin sensitivity. Simvastatin, a lipid‑lowering agent, is contraindicated with cyclosporin and nelfinavir mesylate, highlighting important drug‑interaction considerations. Together, these hierarchical relationships and specific drug‑target interactions guide clinicians in selecting appropriate insulin regimens, oral agents, and combination therapies for safe and effective diabetes management.

title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 275
content: Diabetes pharmacotherapy is organized into two principal groups: insulins and analogues, and non‑insulin blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs. Insulins and analogues encompass all injectable insulin products, including long‑acting formulations such as insulin glargine, the combination product insulin degludec / liraglutide, and the combination product insulin glargine / lixisenatide. These long‑acting injectables are classified under the broader category “insulins and analogues for injection, long‑acting.” Non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents are collectively termed “blood glucose‑lowering drugs, excl. insulins” and are further subdivided by mechanism. Biguanide agents are represented by fenformin, which belongs to the class “biguanide blood glucose‑lowering drugs.” Alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors are another subclass of non‑insulin agents. Dipeptidyl peptidase‑4 (DPP‑4) inhibitors form a distinct class that includes the individual agents alogliptin and sitagliptin as well as the combination product simvastatin / sitagliptin. Glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues constitute an additional subclass and comprise liraglutide and lixisenatide. Both DPP‑4 inhibitors and GLP‑1 analogues are categorized under “blood glucose‑lowering drugs, excl. insulins.” Some therapeutic products combine an insulin with a GLP‑1 analogue, exemplified by insulin degludec / liraglutide (which incorporates liraglutide) and insulin glargine / lixisenatide (which incorporates lixisenatide). These combination agents are members of the long‑acting insulin class while also delivering the GLP‑1 analogue component. Overall, the hierarchy links the overarching “drugs used in diabetes” to its two major branches—insulins and analogues, and non‑insulin glucose‑lowering drugs—each further organized by specific pharmacologic classes and individual agents, reflecting the integrated approach to managing blood glucose in patients with diabetes.

title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 0 -- Community 18
content: Drugs used in diabetes are divided into insulins and analogues and non‑insulin blood‑glucose‑lowering agents. Insulins and analogues include long‑acting injectable formulations such as insulin glargine, insulin glargine/lixisenatide, insulin degludec/liraglutide and the combination product insulin glargine/lixisenatide, which incorporates the GLP‑1 analogue lixisenatide. Non‑insulin glucose‑lowering drugs comprise several therapeutic classes: alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors; dipeptidyl peptidase‑4 (DPP‑4) inhibitors (e.g., alogliptin and sitagliptin, the latter also present in the fixed‑dose combination simvastatin/sitagliptin); glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues (exenatid, liraglutide, lixisenatide); biguanides (fenformin and metformin); and other agents such as benfluorex and repaglinide. Antilipemic agents include bile‑acid sequestrants (e.g., colesevelam) and other compounds such as benfluorex and pirprofen. Simvastatin, a lipid‑lowering drug, is a component of the simvastatin/sitagliptin combination and is contraindicated when co‑administered with nelfinavir mesylate. Colesevelam is contraindicated in patients with pancreatic inflammation (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياس). Recognizing these classifications, combination products, and specific contraindications guides clinicians in selecting appropriate pharmacologic therapy for diabetes and related metabolic disorders while avoiding unsafe drug interactions.
